description: The provided image is an unreadable low-resolution placeholder displaying a 'Check for updates' icon, containing no verifiable scientific data, micrographs, spectra, or diffraction patterns related to the studied steel. Direct pixel-level observation of precipitate morphology, crystal structure, or elemental distribution is impossible. The accompanying text extraction summary indicates that the paper examines co-precipitation in Fe-28Mn-10Al-1C-3Cu austenitic low-density steel after solution treatment at 1000 °C for 1 h and aging at 550 °C for 3 h, referencing figures that should contain SAED patterns, TEM micrographs, EDS maps, and HRTEM images of Cu-rich phase and κ-carbide precipitates. Key text-reported findings include coherent precipitates with specific orientation relationships, lattice parameters (austenite a = 0.3689 nm, κ-carbide a = 0.3775 nm, Cu-rich phase a = 0.3652 nm), and precipitate dimensions (~10 nm irregular ovals and ~30 nm bars), yet none of these details can be visually confirmed from the current image. Consequently, the figure-level scientific conclusions regarding co-precipitation mechanisms remain dependent on caption and text statements rather than direct image evidence.

This figure comprises a high-resolution TEM lattice image and associated diffraction patterns for aged 3Cu steel (Fe-28Mn-10Al-1C-3Cu) after aging at 550 °C for 3 h. The main HRTEM micrograph shows nanoscale lattice fringes of the austenitic matrix (γ) containing co-precipitated κ-carbide (marked γ+κ, yellow dashed box) and Cu-rich phase (marked γ+Cu, red solid box), with a 5 nm scale bar. The yellow-bordered inset diffraction pattern along zone axis Z=[011] exhibits reflections including the (011) superlattice spot and (111), consistent with the ordered L′1₂ structure of κ-carbide. The red-bordered inset pattern along Z=[011] displays spots indexed as (111) and (200), which are characteristic of an FCC crystal structure for the Cu-rich phase. Collectively, these patterns confirm that both precipitates maintain coherency with the austenitic matrix, supporting a cube-on-cube orientation relationship. Although the caption identifies the insets as SAED patterns and a citation sentence reports BCC structure for the Cu-rich phase from SAED analysis, the visibly indexed reflections in the provided red-box pattern correspond to FCC diffraction geometry.

This figure presents transmission electron microscopy characterization of aged 3Cu steel (Fe-28Mn-10Al-1C-3Cu) after solution treatment at 1000 °C for 1 h and aging at 550 °C for 3 h. Panel (a) shows a low-magnification TEM image (scale bar 50 nm) revealing dense intragranular nanoprecipitates. Panel (b) presents EDS elemental maps for Fe, Mn, Al, and Cu, where caption and mapping contrast indicate that the white dashed box corresponds to κ-carbide enriched in Fe, Mn, and Al but depleted in Cu, whereas the magenta-lined region represents Cu-rich phase with Cu enrichment and exclusion of matrix elements. Panel (c) provides a high-resolution TEM image (5 nm scale bar) of an irregular oval-shaped Cu-rich phase, and panel (d) shows a bar-shaped Cu-rich phase with an inset lattice fringe image reporting an interplanar spacing of d = 0.182 nm. Together, these images support the co-precipitation mechanism whereby κ-carbide precipitates first from the austenitic matrix, Cu atoms migrate to the adjacent austenite, and subsequent Cu clustering leads to coherent Cu-rich phase formation.
